retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Nutrient Absorption -- Pathophysiology -- Fat Absorption
retrieved_docs.content: Water-soluble vitamins include thiamine (B1), riboflavin (B2), niacin (B3), pantothenic acid (B5), pyridoxine (B6), biotin (B7), folic acid (B9), cobalamin (B12), and ascorbic acid (C). [49] Water-soluble vitamins do not simply traverse cellular membranes but are absorbed in the digestive tract through specific carrier-mediated pathways. After distribution, they are consumed by the tissues for their metabolic needs.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Water Soluble Vitamins  -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamins play a vital role in many biochemical functions in the human body and are essential components for maintaining optimal health. There are two main groups of vitamins – fat-soluble (easily stored in fat upon absorption) and water-soluble (washed out and not easily stored). Although adequate intake of all vitamins is important, regular intake is required to avoid deficiency due to the transient nature of water-soluble vitamins. The water-soluble vitamins include Vitamin C and Vitamin B complex (thiamine, riboflavin, niacin, pantothenic acid, pyridoxine, biotin, folate, and cobalamin).

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Water Soluble Vitamins  -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin B complex and vitamin C are found in many foods, especially vegetables and fruits, as well as dairy, meat, legumes, peas, liver, eggs, and fortified grains and cereals. In addition to serving as cofactors in biochemical reactions, the vitamin B complex is vital for normal body growth and development, healthy skin, the proper function of nerves and the heart, and red blood cell formation. The overall lack of water-soluble vitamins is rare in North America, though it can present in alcohol use disorder, malabsorption syndromes, strict veganism, and malnourished states.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Fat Soluble Vitamins -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamins are vital micronutrients that cannot be synthesized endogenously or in insufficient amounts, and the principal means by which we get vitamins is through our diet. Vitamins can be classified as water-soluble or fat-soluble. The fat-soluble vitamins include vitamins A, D, E, and K. Fat-soluble vitamins play integral roles in a multitude of physiological processes such as vision, bone health, immune function, and coagulation. This review discusses the biochemistry, transport, and roles of these vitamins, highlighting deficiency syndromes and potential toxicities.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Fat Soluble Vitamins -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: Despite structural differences between fat-soluble vitamins, they are absorbed and transported similarly due to their low solubility in hydrophilic media. The body absorbs fat-soluble vitamins into newly forming micelles in the small intestine. Micelles are lipid clusters that contain hydrophobic groups internally and hydrophilic groups externally. This process relies on the secretion of bile and pancreatic enzymes. After absorption into enterocytes, fat-soluble vitamins become packaged into chylomicrons, which then get secreted into the lymphatic system before entering the bloodstream. Chylomicrons are metabolized by lipoprotein lipase, which causes the release of fat-soluble vitamins into tissues for use and storage.

retrieved_docs.title: Nutrition: Micronutrient Intake, Imbalances, and Interventions -- Definition/Introduction -- Water-Soluble Vitamins
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin B12 (cobalamin): Vitamin B12 is a water-soluble vitamin that is a cofactor in synthesizing DNA, fatty acids, and myelin, as well as erythropoiesis and the development of the nervous system. [7] [29] Bacteria synthesize cobalamin in the gastrointestinal tract of animals, and the host later absorbs it. Because vitamin B12 is concentrated in animal tissues, it is virtually found only in animal products such as meat, dairy, and eggs. [29] [30] The RDA of vitamin B12 for adults is 2.4 mcg/d. [31]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Fat Soluble Vitamins -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: The primary function of vitamin D is to raise plasma calcium and phosphate concentrations, which promotes the mineralization of osteoids in the bone. Elevating calcium levels is necessary for properly functioning the neuromuscular junction, nerve transmission, and secretion and actions of hormones. Vitamin D3 from the skin and vitamin D2 from the diet are prohormones that undergo hydroxylation to 25-hydroxycholecalciferol in the liver via the enzyme 25-hydroxylase. 25-hydroxycholecalciferol becomes further hydroxylated in the kidney to its biologically most active form, 1,25-dihydroxycholecalciferol. Hydroxylation in the kidney into a biologically active form occurs via 1-a-hydroxylase, an enzyme under tight regulation by the parathyroid hormone. The active form of vitamin D increases the duodenal absorption of calcium and phosphate and calcium reabsorption from the distal convoluted tubule by upregulating calcium transporters that move calcium across epithelial cells. Importantly, vitamin D activates osteoclasts, our body’s bone-resorbing cells. The human body maintains equilibrium with bone formation and resorption. To effectively mineralize bone, some level of bone resorption is necessary. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Nutrients -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Nutrients are chemical substances required by the body to sustain basic functions and are optimally obtained by eating a balanced diet. There are six major classes of nutrients essential for human health: carbohydrates, lipids, proteins, vitamins, minerals, and water. Carbohydrates, lipids, and proteins are considered macronutrients and serve as a source of energy. Water is required in large amounts but does not yield energy. Vitamins and minerals are considered micronutrients and play essential roles in metabolism. Vitamins are organic micronutrients classified as either water-soluble or fat-soluble. The essential water-soluble vitamins include vitamins B1, B2, B3, B5, B6, B7, B9, B12, and C. The essential fat-soluble vitamins include vitamins A, E, D, and K. Minerals are inorganic micronutrients. Minerals can classify as macrominerals or microminerals. Macrominerals are required in amounts greater than 100 mg per day and include calcium, phosphorous, magnesium, sodium, potassium, and chloride. Sodium, potassium, and chloride are also electrolytes. Microminerals are those nutrients required in amounts less than 100 mg per day and include iron, copper, zinc, selenium, and iodine. This article will review the following biochemical aspects of the essential nutrients: fundamentals, cellular, molecular, function, testing, and clinical significance. [1] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Lipoprotein Metabolism -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Lipids are one of the four main biological molecules of the human body, along with carbohydrates, proteins, and nucleic acids. Lipids are essential components of life on a cellular level. They are involved in multiple processes such as storing energy, serving as chemical messengers, forming cell membranes, and transporting fat-soluble vitamins such as vitamin E. For lipids to carry out these roles in the cell, they must travel to their destination cells after being absorbed in the gastrointestinal tract. Without lipoproteins, this transport would not be possible, as the hydrophilic environment of the blood is not compatible with the hydrophobic nature of lipids like cholesterol. Therefore, lipoproteins play an integral role in the ability of the human body to utilize lipids, and the metabolism of these lipoproteins has a direct effect on the level of lipids in the serum and on the subsequent processes that involve lipids within the cell.

retrieved_docs.title: Vitamin D Toxicity -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin D is a fat-soluble vitamin stored in the liver and adipose tissue. It is available from exogenous sources and synthesized endogenously by the human body. The Endocrine Society clinical practice guidelines suggest that the daily requirement of vitamin D for adults aged 19 to 50 years is 600 IU/d. For those aged 50 to 70, the daily requirement is at least 600 IU/d. For those older than 70, the daily requirement is at least 800 IU/d. The maximum suggested daily requirement is 4000 IU/d for everyone older than 8 years. [14] Vitamin D is metabolically inactive and requires a series of metabolic transformations to become its active form. This includes forms acquired from external sources or through endogenous synthesis.

retrieved_docs.title: Vitamin B6 (Pyridoxine) -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin B6 (pyridoxine) is a water-soluble substance that converts inside the body into essential coenzymes for more than 100 enzymes in the human body.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Water Soluble Vitamins  -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamins are classified into two categories based on how they are absorbed and if they are stored. Water-soluble vitamins dissolve in water upon entering the body. Because of this, humans cannot store excess amounts of water-soluble vitamins for later use. There are nine water-soluble vitamins: the B vitamins -- folate, thiamine, riboflavin, niacin, pantothenic acid, biotin, vitamin B6, and vitamin B12 -- and vitamin C. Deficiency of any of these water-soluble vitamins results in a clinical syndrome that may result in severe morbidity and mortality.

retrieved_docs.title: Nutrition: Micronutrient Intake, Imbalances, and Interventions -- Definition/Introduction -- Water-Soluble Vitamins
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin B5 (pantothenic acid): Vitamin B5 is of fatty acid synthase and coenzyme component A, which are needed for cell growth, energy production, and hormone synthesis. [7] [24] Pantothenic acid is found in eggs, milk, vegetables, beef, chicken, whole grains, and fortified foods. The RDA is 5 mg/d for adult men and women. The RDA for pregnant and lactating women is 6 mg/d and 7 mg/d, respectively. [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Nutrients -- Fundamentals -- Vitamin A (Retinol)
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin A, or retinol, is an essential fat-soluble vitamin that plays numerous roles in vision, cellular differentiation, gene expression, growth, the immune system, bone development, and reproduction. Vitamin A is found primarily in animal products. Fruits and vegetables are a source of provitamin A carotenoids that can be converted to retinol in the body at a lesser amount. The RDA for vitamin A for adults is 900 mcg/day for males and 700 mcg/day for females. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Nutrients -- Fundamentals -- Vitamin B6 (Pyridoxine)
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin B6, or pyridoxine, is an essential water-soluble vitamin that acts as a coenzyme for amino acid, glycogen, and sphingoid base metabolism. Vitamin B6 is widespread among food groups. The RDA for vitamin B6 for adults is 1.3 mg/day. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Vitamin E -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: The primary mechanism of action of alpha-tocopherol is as a scavenger of free radicals. It binds to free radicals, protecting cellular components from oxidative damage. Peroxidation of polyunsaturated fatty acids in the cell membrane from free radicals leads to cellular injury. Vitamin E plays a protective role. It also inhibits the aggregation of platelets, monocyte adhesion, and proliferation of cells. [1] [4] Vitamin E is hepatically metabolized and is primarily excreted in the bile and feces but also the urine. It is also stored in the body's adipose tissues.

Collectively, the community data demonstrate extensive utilization of vitamin A, niacin (vitamin B3), calcium pantothenate (vitamin B5), vitamin D and vitamin E across oral, topical, parenteral and nutritional formulations. These micronutrients interact to support cellular differentiation, enzymatic processes, antioxidant protection, energy metabolism, red‑blood‑cell formation, nervous‑system health, bone metabolism and overall patient outcomes, providing clinicians and formulators with an integrated framework for designing comprehensive micronutrient regimens.

Vitamin K, another fat‑soluble vitamin, is present in multivitamin‑with‑iron tablets, demonstrating that combination products can deliver both water‑soluble B‑complex vitamins (niacin, calcium pantothenate) and fat‑soluble vitamins (A, D, K). Retinoids are correctly classified as a subclass of the vitamin A family rather than vitamin K, and pharmacologic retinoid agents such as Palovarotene (an inverse retinoid) are mentioned alongside vitamin A preparations that are often co‑formulated with vitamin D. Collectively, these relationships depict a hierarchical organization of nutrients—vitamin A family, vitamin B‑complex/B‑vitamin family, and fat‑soluble vitamins (A, D, K)—and illustrate how multivitamin‑mineral supplements integrate multiple active and inactive components to achieve comprehensive nutrient coverage. Understanding each component’s active versus inactive status, biochemical function, therapeutic role (e.g., bone health, metabolic support) and safety

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 2120
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin K is classified as a fat‑soluble vitamin, meaning it belongs to the broader group of vitamins that dissolve in lipids and require dietary fat for optimal absorption. This places vitamin K within the same category as vitamins A, D, and E, highlighting shared characteristics such as storage in fatty tissues and the need for adequate fat intake to ensure proper bioavailability. Recognizing vitamin K as a fat‑soluble vitamin is essential for understanding its absorption dynamics, its role in physiological processes like blood coagulation, and for guiding nutritional recommendations that support its effectiveness.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 2041
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin K 2 and vitamin K 3 are both classified as vitamins. As members of the vitamin group, they are directly related to نقص التغذية (nutritional deficiency), highlighting that insufficient intake or absorption of these vitamins can contribute to a state of nutritional deficiency. Recognizing vitamin K 2 and vitamin K 3 within the broader context of vitamins is essential for identifying and preventing nutrient‑related health problems.

Collectively, these fat‑soluble (vitamins A, D) and water‑soluble B‑vitamins (niacin, calcium pantothenate) form a coordinated network that appears across oral, sublingual, topical and nutraceutical products, especially in prenatal, diabetic, bariatric and general multivitamin regimens. Their combined physiological effects—antioxidant activity, enzyme modulation, cellular metabolism, lipid regulation and bone‑protective actions—underscore their importance for nutritional adequacy, metabolic health and patient‑centered care.

Finally, vitamin A (plain) and vitamin D (and analogues) are grouped under “vitamin A and D, including combinations of the two,” while vitamin B₁ (plain) belongs to the class “vitamin B₁, plain and in combination with vitamin B₆ and B₁₂,” reflecting the availability of both single‑nutrient and combination products. This structured representation integrates nutrient taxonomy, mineral‑vitamin interactions, deficiency states, and related pharmacologic agents, providing a comprehensive framework for patient nutrition and therapeutic management.

retrieved_docs.content: products and primarily inhibits bone resorption, underscoring its importance for skeletal health. Collectively, vitamin A, niacin (vitamin B3), calcium pantothenate (vitamin B5) and vitamin D are integral components of many oral multivitamin‑mineral formulations, each serving specific nutritional or therapeutic roles—vitamin A as an antioxidant and enzyme regulator, niacin and calcium pantothenate as essential B‑vitamins with metabolic interconnections, and vitamin D as a regulator of bone metabolism—while vitamin A and niacin may also be present as inactive excipients in certain topical products. This integrated composition supports diverse patient‑care needs ranging from general nutrition and prenatal health to disease‑specific supplementation (e.g., diabetes, iron deficiency) and bone‑health maintenance.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 1 -- Community 177
retrieved_docs.content: biotin, calcium pantothenate), vitamin C (ascorbic acid), vitamin D (cholecalciferol) often paired with calcium sources (e.g., calcium oyster shell) to support calcium metabolism and inhibit bone resorption, vitamin E (α‑tocopherol), and essential minerals (iron, calcium, magnesium, zinc, copper, iodine, selenium, chromium, molybdenum, vanadium, potassium iodide). Many formulations also contain omega‑3 fatty acids and antioxidants such as lutein and α‑lipoic acid. Collectively, vitamin A, niacin and calcium pantothenate function both independently and synergistically across diverse pharmaceutical and nutraceutical products. Their combined actions provide antioxidant protection, metabolic and lipid regulation, enzymatic support, immune competence, red‑blood‑cell formation, bone mineralization and overall nutritional adequacy, underscoring their broad relevance to patient care, therapeutic formulation design and clinical outcomes.

Recognizing niacin’s role within the B‑vitamin family and distinguishing it from other B vitamins such as pantothenic acid is essential for accurate formulation labeling and for clinicians advising patients on supplement use.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 177
retrieved_docs.content: The community groups all vitamin‑related agents under the class “vitaminy,” linking them to nutritional deficiency and to a broader chemical domain. Fat‑soluble vitamins form a sub‑hierarchy that includes vitamin A (retinoids), vitamin D (cholecalciferol, kalcifediol = 25‑hydroxy‑vitamin D, and kalcitriol = 1,25‑dihydroxy‑vitamin D), vitamin E (α‑tocoferol and related tokoferols), and vitamin K, while water‑soluble vitamins comprise vitamin C (ascorbic acid) and the B‑complex (thiamine B₁, riboflavin B₂, niacinamide B₃, pyridoxine B₆, cyanocobalamin B₁₂, hydroxokobalamin, mecobalamin, and folic acid) together with their combination products. Multivitamin preparations are child concepts of “vitaminy” and illustrate co‑administration of multiple vitamins and essential minerals; examples include plain multivitamin tablets, liquid multivitamins such as IRON/MULTIVITAMS LIQUID, and prenatal multivitamins containing vitamin A acetate, β‑carotene, ascorbic acid, cholecalciferol, α‑tocopherol
